title-group.article-title: Effect of silica‐sprayed collection tubes on synovial fluid bacterial culture

author-notes.corresp: *CorrespondenceP. Jimenez Rihuete, Sussex Equine Hospital, Billinghurst RD, Ashington RH203BB, UK. Email: menezpablo@gmail.com

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2024 The Author(s). Veterinary Medicine and Science published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Silica‐sprayed tubes (SSTs) are often used to transport synovial fluid samples in equine practice. They promote the coagulation of the sample. The objective of the study is to evaluate the effect of SST on bacterial culture.

sec.title: Materials and methods
sec.p: The study was divided into two parts: sterile saline (Part A) and synovial fluid (Part B). Four common bacteria associated with equine synovial sepsis were used: Streptococcus pyogenes, Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus and methicillin‐resistant S. aureus (MRSA). Three collection tubes were used: STT, plain (no‐additives) and brain and heart infusion (BHI) broth. Bacteria were cultured in horse blood agar plates for 48 h. Outcome variables were negative culture, positive culture and total number of colony‐forming units (CFUs). Statistical analysis was performed using Mann–Whitney U test, and significance was set at p < 0.05.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The total number of agar plates read was 1557 (779 saline; 778 synovial fluid). Total negative cultures were 25/779 on saline and 3/778 on synovial fluid. In broth, maximum growth CFU was achieved after 8 h for both saline and synovial fluid for all bacteria. S. pyogenesand E. coli produced a significantly lower number of CFU when in SST compared to plain or broth after 4 h, whereas S. aureus (American Type Culture Collection [ATCC] and MRSA) only after 24 h.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Silica‐containing tubes reduced bacterial proliferation, whereas the use of a BHI broth provided the highest bacterial load in the sample. The use of SST may have a negative effect on bacterial proliferation in samples obtained from clinical cases.

abstract.p: The capacity of survival differs between bacterial species. Silica has a negative effect on bacterial survival and may negatively affect the total number of positive bacterial cultures. Using a BHI broth may offer a higher chance of obtaining a positive bacterial culture. Having positive culture will provide better antimicrobial stewardship; the results of this study may therefore contribute to setting new standard procedures for sample collection on equine synovial fluid when the suspicion of synovial sepsis is present.

p.mixed-citation: Jimenez Rihuete, P., Martin, C., Villarino, N. F., & Rubio‐Martínez, L. M. (2024). Effect of silica‐sprayed collection tubes on synovial fluid bacterial culture. Veterinary Medicine and Science, 10, e1501. 10.1002/vms3.1501

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Synovial sepsis is one of the most common morbidities and mortalities of horses admitted to equine referral hospitals worldwide (de Souza et al., 2021). Bacterial culture and antimicrobial sensitivity testing are an important part of the diagnosis and treatment selection of synovial infections (Johns & Adams, 2015; Richardson & Stewart, 2019). Positive bacterial culture of equine synovial fluid is the gold standard for diagnosing synovial sepsis. However, as low as 37.5% positive cultures have previously been reported on clinical cases with a presumptive diagnosis of synovial sepsis (Crosby et al., 2023; Hepworth‐Warren et al., 2015; Madison et al., 1991; Meijer et al., 2000; Steel, 2008). The presence of synovial sepsis with a positive culture has been associated with a poor prognosis (de Souza et al., 2021; Taylor et al., 2010). This might be related to higher bacterial loads being more likely to produce positive cultures whilst causing persistent, uncontrolled synovial sepsis and therefore be associated with a worse outcome. The emergence of antimicrobial resistance requires an early identification of pathogens for adequate antimicrobial selection (Chipangura et al., 2017; Miagkoff et al., 2020; Raidal, 2019).

Silica‐sprayed tubes (SSTs) are often used to collect and transport synovial fluid samples in equine practice (Cowell et al., 1987). SSTs are designed for blood sample collection and have the goal of maximising the amount of serum whilst minimising the presence of residual fibrinogen within the serum, which is known to be detrimental for laboratory instruments (Gryshchuk & Galagan, 2016; Lima‐Oliveira et al., 2021). This occurs because silica is negatively charged, which initiates the intrinsic coagulation pathway (Ivanov et al., 2017), resulting in cell agglutination within the clot (Mudge, 2019; Neubauer & Zieger, 2022). As bacterial proliferation occurs following adhesion with the cells of the infected host (Nercessian & Busalmen, 2022; Słotwińska, 2013), it is possible that the use of SST may have a negative effect on bacterial culture as bacteria may get sequestered within the clot (Whang, 2010). Silica may also negatively affect bacteria directly; Masuki et al., 2020 showed that silica is cytotoxic to specific human cells. To the best of our knowledge, the effect of the presence of silica on bacterial culture is unknown. The use of broths has previously been shown to increase the percentage of positive cultures on different sample types in many species in comparison with direct bacterial culture (Heng et al., 2022; Nocera et al., 2021).

The objective of the study is to compare the effect of collection tubes containing silica crystals, with plain and, brain and heart broth, on bacterial proliferation and culture in sterile saline and equine synovial fluid. Our hypotheses are as follows: (1) SST yields a lower number of colony‐forming units (CFU) than plain tubes in both saline and synovial fluid. (2) SST yields a lower total number of CFU in synovial fluid than in saline. (3) SST yields a lower total number of CFU in synovial fluid than in brain and heart infusion (BHI) broth.

sec.title: MATERIALS AND METHODS
sec.p:
The study was divided into two parts based on the medium used for bacterial exposure to different coagulation tubes: sterile saline (Vetivex 1, sodium chloride 9 mg/mL, Dechra, Sansaw Business Park) (Part A) and synovial fluid (Part B).
Part A: One millilitre of sterile saline was placed in STT and plain tubes. One millilitre of sterile BHI broth (Thermo Fisher Scientific) was placed in a plain tube.
Part B: One millilitre of synovial fluid was placed in STT and plain tubes. In addition, 0.5 mL of sterile BHI broth (Thermo Fisher Scientific) and 0.5 mL of synovial fluid were placed in a plain tube. The synovial fluid was obtained by needle aspiration from the tarsocrural, antebrachiocarpal and middle carpal joints from five horses euthanised at a local abattoir. The joints were clipped and scrubbed with chlorhexidine gluconate and surgical spirit prior to synoviocentesis, which was performed under strict sterile conditions. According to the medical records, the donor horses did not receive antibiotics in the month prior to euthanasia. All synovial fluid samples were pooled immediately after collection. Synovial fluid analysis was performed at the hospital's laboratory by one of the authors immediately upon collection. Nucleated cell count (InSight V5 Haematology Analyser. Woodley Equipment Company Ltd.) was ≤1 × 109 c/L, and total protein ≤20 g/L (Master refractometer, Atago Co LTD, The Front Tower Shiba Koen). A bacterial culture of the synovial fluid was performed immediately on five Columbia horse blood agar plates (E&O Laboratories Ltd, Burnhouse) for 24 h, obtaining a negative result in all plates. Plain (BD Vacutainer plus (no additive) tube with Hemogard, Becton, Dickinson and Company Belliver Industrial Estate) and SST (BD Vacutainer Serum plus tube with Hemogard, Becton, Dickinson and Company Belliver Industrial Estate) polyethylene terephthalate collection tubes were used.

Four bacteria strains commonly associated with equine synovial sepsis (Gilbertie et al., 2018) were used. These included three bacterial strains of American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Langford Veterinary Services Ltd, Diagnostic Laboratories): Streptococcus pyogenes, Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus); and a single wild strain of methicillin‐resistant S. aureus (MRSA) obtained in‐house from clinical equine patients presented to the equine hospital. The wild MRSA was characterized following Gram positive and catalyse positive testing (Lowy, 1998), latex positive result (Essers & Radebold, 1980) and plated with a cefoxitin disc diffusion in identification of the methicillin resistance (Panda et al., 2016). All bacteria underwent fluorescence‐based biochemical testing (Vitek, Langford Veterinary Services Ltd, Diagnostic Laboratories) prior to and at the end of the study to ensure that the correct bacteria had been cultured throughout the study (Ligozzi et al., 2002; Crowley et al., 2012).

Each bacteria strain was plated following a standard laboratory bacterial spread technique (Sanders, 2012) and was subsequently cultured on Columbia horse blood agar plates (E&O laboratories Ltd) for 24 h. For part A, a single bacterial CFU from each bacteria strain was diluted in 20 mL of sterile saline (Vetivex 1, sodium chloride 9 mg/mL, Dechra), obtaining a reduced number of bacteria/mL. The same procedure was performed for part B using 20 mL of the pooled synovial fluid mentioned previously. From these dilutions, as control, 1 μL from each CFU dilution was inoculated 15 times on Columbia horse blood agar plates. For the test, for each part, 50 μL was inoculated into the collection tubes, separately for Parts A and B. This was repeated five times per bacteria strain (Figure 1). All collection tubes were maintained at room temperature 21°C (+/− 1.5°C), emulating the conditions of transport of the sample to the laboratory for 30 min, 4 h, 8 h and 24 h. Obtaining a total of 1560 inoculated plates.

caption.p: Methodology dilution for both parts: (A) sterile saline and (B) synovial fluid.

sec.p: Plates were assigned an alphanumeric randomised code for each bacterium, collection tube and time of collection, prior to being cultured aerobically on for 24 h. The first author read all plates blindly, recording the CFU assigned to each code. The results were recorded as negative (no visible CFU to the naked eye) or positive (bacterial growth). In the positive ones, the total number of CFU up to 250 was recorded. A number of CFU ≥ 250 was recorded as 250. If the result was negative at 24 h, the plates were cultured for a further 24 h to ensure that no growth was obtained. If a positive culture was recorded, the plate was discarded.

One plate for each bacterium from both parts A and B of the study (three ATCC types and ‘wild’ MRSA, eight in total) was selected randomly by our in‐house microbiologist and tested via Vitex to confirm fluorescence‐based biochemical matching for each bacterium across the study.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Mann–Whitney U test was used to compare the median CFU of each bacterium of two independent groups at each time point. The compared groups were (1) silica collecting tube with added saline vs. plain collecting tube with added saline, (2) silica collecting tube with synovial fluid vs. plain collecting tube with synovial fluid, (3) silica collecting tube saline vs. silica collecting tube with synovial fluid and (4) silica collecting tube with synovial fluid vs. BH broth tube with synovial fluid. Statistical analysis was performed via GraphPad Prism 7.04. The significance level was set at <0.05. The results were expressed in median with ranges.

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: For part A, a total of 779 plates were read. A total of 25 negative and 754 positive results were obtained. For part B, a total of 778 plates were read. A total of 3 negative and 775 positive results were obtained. Three plates were discarded due to dislodging of the agar, one from part A and two from part B. For both parts, the plates that showed a negative result were cultured for a total of 48 h, and no growth was obtained on any of them.

All controls reached a total of 250 CFU for both parts A and B. All bacteria tested via Vitex showed fluorescence‐based biochemical matching for each bacterium across the study, showing no cross‐contamination for the randomly selected plates for each bacterium.

In BHI, a total number of 250 CFU was achieved at 8 h for both saline and synovial fluid for all bacteria. In plain tubes, S. pyogenes did not achieve a total number of 250 CFU at any time; E. coli achieved a total number of 250 CFU at 24 h in saline but not in synovial fluid; S. aureus and MRSA achieved a total number of 250 CFU at 24 h in synovial fluid but not in saline. In SST, neither S. pyogenes nor E. coli achieved a total number of 250 CFU at any time, but S. pyogenes yielded a statistically significant lower number of CFU in saline in SST when compared to plain at 0.5 h 76 (32–192)/56 (22–143), at 4 h 29 (14–129)/54 (19–92), at 8 h 18 (3–47)/29 (15–55) and at 24 h 0 (0–2)/8 (0–18) (Figure 2).

caption.p: Results of colony‐forming unit (CFU) for Streptococcus pyogenes expressed in median with ranges. Y axis as total CFU, X axis as different hours for container and medium.

sec.p: E. coli yielded a statistically significantly lower number of CFU in saline and synovial fluid in SST when compared to plain at 24 h (Saline 232 (74–250)/250 (250–250), synovial fluid 4 (0–8)/35 (6–98)) (Figure 3).

caption.p: Results of colony‐forming unit (CFU) for Escherichia coli expressed in median with ranges. Y axis as total CFU, X axis as different hours for container and medium.

sec.p: S. aureus showed a statistically significant lower number of CFU in saline but not in synovial fluid in SST when compared to plain at 24 h (Saline 54 (30–156)/37 (22–82), synovial fluid 250 (250–250)/250 (250–250)). In synovial fluid in SST a statistically significantly lower number of CFU was seen when compared to BHI, only after 8 h (89 (48–146)/250 (78–250)) (Figure 4).

caption.p: Results of colony‐forming unit (CFU) for Staphylococcus aureus expressed in median with ranges. Y axis as total CFU, X axis as different hours for container and medium.

sec.p: MRSA did not show statistically significant differences in the number of CFU between SST and plain in either saline or synovial fluid at any time. However, a statistically significantly higher number of CFU was seen in SST in saline when compared to synovial fluid at 24 h (93 (0–134)/250 (250–250)) (Figure 5).

caption.p: Results of colony‐forming unit (CFU) for methicillin‐resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) expressed in median with ranges. Y axis as total CFU, X axis as different hours for container and medium.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: SSTs showed a lower total number of CFU when compared to plain and BHI broth. There were individual variations for each bacterium, but they all showed a maximum number of CFU at a faster rate (8 h) on BHI than in other collection media.

Staphylococcus spp. is one of the most commonly reported bacteria associated with synovial sepsis in horses (Gilbertie et al., 2018; de Souza et al., 2021). These studies do not report collection or culture methods. However, Staphylococcus spp. did resist exposure to all collection tubes and replicated in greater numbers and faster than the other bacterium types in this study. This potentially reflects this bacteria's survival capability under laboratory conditions but not its pathogenesis. It is, therefore, possible that the reported total incidence of synovial sepsis caused by Staphylococcus spp. may be over‐represented due to a smaller number of false negative results.

In this study, SST yielded lower CFU in synovial fluid than in saline for all bacteria except for E. coli. E. coli showed a decrease in the total number of CFU over time in synovial fluid in SST but not in plain tubes. This could be the result of silica causing sequestration of bacteria within the clot, through cell agglutination, which may decrease bacteria proliferation under laboratory conditions (Letourneau et al., 2011). As bacterial proliferation is highly dependent on bacterial adhesion to the cell membrane, it is plausible to consider that the highest bacterial load within the synovial fluid sample may reside within the clotted part of the sample, rather than the fluid (Słotwińska et al., 2013). A similar effect on bacteria may occur; however, further research is required.

The use of broths has previously been shown to increase the total number of positive cultures in other equine samples (Nocera et al., 2021). In other publications, the use of BHI was deemed essential for obtaining a positive bacterial culture (Koziy et al., 2019). Multiple growing media were compared, including automated blood culture methods and thioglycolate broth, showing superior positive results with the automated blood culture (Dumoulin et al., 2010). These are, however, expensive and require laboratory machines not commonly available for the veterinary industry. In this study, BHI yielded the highest CFU at 8 h for all types of bacteria. Therefore, BHI may be recommended to increase the likelihood of positive bacterial culture in synovial fluid samples.

Cases with suspected synovial sepsis often receive systemic antimicrobials prior to referral, where synoviocentesis is often repeated at a hospital setting. Previous administration of antimicrobials may have a negative effect on bacterial replication and the chance of obtaining a positive culture (McEwen & Collignon, 2018). An effort should be made to submit for culture the first sample obtained before the administration of antimicrobials. Use of SST may have an additional negative effect on bacterial culture, which would further reduce the likelihood of obtaining a positive culture on cases with presumptive synovial sepsis that have received antimicrobials. Future studies on the effect of SST on synovial fluid samples from clinical cases should be performed.

This study has some limitations. The sampled synovial fluid, used as media, was reported to not have antimicrobials based on animal information available; however, this was not tested. This study is based on an ex vivo contamination of synovial fluid which is not necessarily the same as in clinical cases. For the purpose of this study, we standardised the volume and colony dilution ratio according to prior testing, which may differ from an expected variety of bacterial burdens in clinical cases. ATCC strains may differ from wild‐type bacteria in the rate of replication and laboratory results. Ex vivo contamination of synovial fluid in laboratory settings does not stimulate the inflammatory response obtained in an articular septic process. In clinical cases, the host inflammatory reaction would decrease the total bacterial burden; the fibrinocellular conglomerate may reduce the number of bacteria within the sampled synovial fluid (Madison et al., 1991; Milner et al., 2014). This may reduce even further the likelihood of obtaining a positive culture. It is possible that a higher concentration of silica per total bacteria inoculated could be associated with a lower probability of obtaining a positive culture result.

In clinical practice, collection methods that ensure a higher rates of bacterial recovery may increase the chances of obtaining a positive culture. In this study, for all bacteria, the use of BHI broth yielded far higher numbers of CFU, with all samples reaching maximal growth at 8 h, even at room temperature. Contrarily, SST reduced the total CFU overtime. It can, therefore, be concluded that silica has a negative effect on bacterial survival and may negatively affect the total number of positive bacterial cultures in clinical cases. Using a BHI broth may offer a higher chance of obtaining a positive bacterial culture by favouring bacterial growth in synovial fluid samples from horses. Having positive culture will provide better antimicrobial stewardship; the results of this study may therefore contribute to setting new standard procedures for sample collection.

sec.title: AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS
sec.p: Conceptualization; data curation; formal analysis; funding acquisition; investigation; methodology; project administration; writing—original draft: Pablo Jimenez Rihuete. Investigation; methodology; writing—review and editing: Carolynn Martin. Formal analysis; writing—review and editing: Nicolas Villarino. Conceptualization; supervision; writing—review and editing: Luis M. Rubio Martinez.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors whose names are listed have no affiliations with or involvement in any organisation or entity with any financial interest in the subject matter or materials discussed in this manuscript.

sec.title: ETHICS STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors confirm that the ethical policies of the journal, as noted on the journal's author guidelines page, have been adhered to. No ethical approval was required as this is a review article with no original research data.

sec.title: PEER REVIEW
sec.p: The peer review history for this paper is available at https://publons.com/publon/10.1002/vms3.1501.

ack.title: ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS
ack.p: This study is supported by GVMB research fund.

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
